Prior ProvisionsProvisions similar to those in this section were contained in , , which superseded R.S. §§ 2766 and Section 401 of the 1922 act was superseded by section 401 of act , comprising this section, and repealed by section 651(a)(1) of the 1930 Act.act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, title IV, § 40142 Stat. 948June 17, 1930Section III of the Underwood Tariff , , amending the Customs Administrative , , was repealed by , title IV, .Act of Oct. 3, 1913, ch. 1638 Stat. 181Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 40726 Stat. 131section 643 of the act of Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 35642 Stat. 989Section III, by subdivision A thereof, amended the Customs Administrative , , as previously amended, to read as set forth in section III, subdivisions B–CC. By that amendment and reenactment, the Customs Administrative Act of , and the amendments thereof by , , , , , , , , and the Payne-Aldrich Tariff , , were superseded, except the provisions thereof mentioned in a proviso of section IV, S, of that act.Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 40726 Stat. 131act July 24, 1897, ch. 11, § 3230 Stat. 211act May 17, 1898, ch. 34130 Stat. 417Act Dec. 15, 1902, ch. 132 Stat. 753act May 27, 1908, ch. 20535 Stat. 403Act of Aug. 5, 1909, ch. 6, § 2836 Stat. 91June 10, 1890The Customs Administrative Act of , as originally enacted and as amended previous to the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, consisted of thirty sections, of which section 30 prescribed the time when the act should go into effect. Of the preceding twenty-nine sections of the original act, section 15 providing for review by the courts of decisions of the Board of General Appraisers, was omitted from the act as further amended by the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, and the remaining twenty-eight sections were amended thereby, constituting sections 1–28 thereof. A new section, designated as section 29, was added by the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, which created a Court of Customs Appeals and prescribed its jurisdiction and powers, proceedings, etc. Its provisions were incorporated in and superseded by chapter 8 of the Judicial Code of . Another new section, designated as section 30, was also added by the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, which provided for the appointment of an Assistant Attorney-General, a Deputy Assistant Attorney-General, and attorneys, in charge of matters of reappraisement, etc., of imported goods and litigation incident thereto. Section 30 was incorporated into the Code as section 296 of former Title 5, Executive Departments and Government Officers and Employees, and subsequently repealed by , , § 8(a), .June 10, 1890March 3, 1911Sept. 6, 1966Pub. L. 89–55480 Stat. 632

children.heading: Vessel
children.label: a Vessel
children.text: The word “vessel” includes every description of water craft or other contrivance used, or capable of being used, as a means of transportation in water, but does not include aircraft.

children.heading: Vehicle
children.label: b Vehicle
children.text: The word “vehicle” includes every description of carriage or other contrivance used, or capable of being used, as a means of transportation on land, but does not include aircraft.

children.heading: Merchandise
children.label: c Merchandise
children.text: The word “merchandise” means goods, wares, and chattels of every description, and includes merchandise the importation of which is prohibited, and monetary instruments as defined in .section 5312 of title 31

children.heading: Person
children.label: d Person
children.text: The word “person” includes partnerships, associations, and corporations.

children.heading: Master
children.label: e Master
children.text: The word “master” means the person having the command of the vessel.

children.heading: Day
children.label: f Day
children.text: The word “day” means the time from eight o’clock antemeridian to five o’clock postmeridian.

children.heading: Night
children.label: g Night
children.text: The word “night” means the time from five o’clock postmeridian to eight o’clock antemeridian.

children.heading: United States
children.label: h United States
children.text: The term “United States” includes all Territories and possessions of the United States except the Virgin Islands, American Samoa, Wake Island, Midway Islands, Kingman Reef, Johnston Island, and the island of Guam.

children.heading: Officer of the customs; customs officer
children.label: i Officer of the customs; customs officer
children.text: The terms “officer of the customs” and “customs officer” mean any officer of the United States Customs Service of the Treasury Department (also hereinafter referred to as the “Customs Service”) or any commissioned, warrant, or petty officer of the Coast Guard, or any agent or other person, including foreign law enforcement officers, authorized by law or designated by the Secretary of the Treasury to perform any duties of an officer of the Customs Service.

children.heading: Customs waters
children.label: j Customs waters
children.text: The term “customs waters” means, in the case of a foreign vessel subject to a treaty or other arrangement between a foreign government and the United States enabling or permitting the authorities of the United States to board, examine, search, seize, or otherwise to enforce upon such vessel upon the high seas the laws of the United States, the waters within such distance of the coast of the United States as the said authorities are or may be so enabled or permitted by such treaty or arrangement and, in the case of every other vessel, the waters within four leagues of the coast of the United States.

any vessel which is found or kept off the coast of the United States within or without the customs waters, if, from the history, conduct, character, or location of the vessel, it is reasonable to believe that such vessel is being used or may be used to introduce or promote or facilitate the introduction or attempted introduction of merchandise into the United States in violation of the laws of the United States; and
any vessel which has visited a vessel described in paragraph (1).

children.heading: Secretary
children.label: l Secretary
children.text: The term “Secretary” means the Secretary of the Treasury or his delegate.
an appropriate license or permit; or
the Controlled Substances Import and Export Act [ et seq.].21 U.S.C. 951

children.heading: Electronic transmission
children.label: n Electronic transmission
children.text: The term “electronic transmission” means the transfer of data or information through an authorized electronic data interchange system consisting of, but not limited to, computer modems and computer networks.
entry information required for the entry of merchandise, and
entry summary information required for the classification and appraisement of the merchandise, the verification of statistical information, and the determination of compliance with applicable law.

children.heading: Electronic data interchange system
children.label: p Electronic data interchange system
children.text: The term “electronic data interchange system” means any established mechanism approved by the Commissioner of U.S. Customs and Border Protection through which information can be transferred electronically.

children.heading: National Customs Automation Program
children.label: q National Customs Automation Program
children.text: The term “National Customs Automation Program” means the program established under .section 1411 of this title

children.heading: Import activity summary statement
children.label: r Import activity summary statement
children.text: The term “import activity summary statement” refers to data or information transmitted electronically to the Customs Service, in accordance with such regulations as the Secretary prescribes, at the end of a specified period of time which enables the Customs Service to assess properly the duties, taxes and fees on merchandise imported during that period, collect accurate statistics and determine whether any other applicable requirement of law (other than a requirement relating to release from customs custody) is met.

children.heading: Reconciliation
children.label: s Reconciliation
children.text: The term “reconciliation” means an electronic process, initiated at the request of an importer, under which the elements of an entry (other than those elements related to the admissibility of the merchandise) that are undetermined at the time the importer files or transmits the documentation or information required by , or the import activity summary statement, are provided to the Customs Service at a later time. A reconciliation is treated as an entry for purposes of liquidation, reliquidation, recordkeeping, and protest.section 1484(a)(1)(B) of this title

children.heading: Reconfigured entry
children.label: t Reconfigured entry
children.text: The term “reconfigured entry” means an entry filed on an import activity summary statement which substitutes for all or part of 1 or more entries filed under or filed on a reconciliation entry that aggregates the entry elements to be reconciled under for purposes of liquidation, reliquidation, or protest.section 1484(a)(1)(A) of this titlesection 1484(b) of this title
